name: Jeroen
title: Senior Data Analyst and Power BI Developer
description: Portfolio website of Jeroen - Senior Data Analyst and Power BI Developer
aboutMe: As a driven data analyst and Power BI developer, I believe that teamwork, organization, and flexibility are the key ingredients for success. My core skills include data analysis, data visualization engineering, and data discovery and modeling, using AI where it has its place. While business decisions can often be based on gut feeling, why not back them up with data?
Data only drives action when it connects with human cognition. I design data visualizations based on cognitive design principles, making insights instinctual, decisions faster, and impact bigger.

featuredWork.title: True Scale Data Viz: The Wild Bee Crisis
featuredWork.summary: The Micro world: A dynamic, physical ruler that shows four wild bee species - from the Tiny Furrow Bee 6mm to the Buff-tailed Bumblebee 22mm - exactly 1:1 on true scale on your screen.
featuredWork.description:
When people think of "bee extinction", they almost always think of the honeybee. But honeybees are basically like farm animals. They are taken care of and fed by beekeepers.

The real crisis is happening in silence to our wild bees. The Netherlands has over 350 species of wild bees and bumblebees. They don’t have a beekeeper to look after them, and their numbers have dropped massively since 1950.

For a personal Power BI report, I wanted to make this hidden micro-world visible. Every now and then I check in on Kerry Kolosko's work as her creativity is unmatched. Inspired by one of her projects, I built an interactive canvas in Power BI using HTML/SVG where two things come together:

1. The Macro trend: An area chart showing the decline of our wild bee population since 1950. (Purposely honeybees are not included here!).

2. The Micro world: A dynamic, physical ruler that shows four wild bee species - from the Tiny Furrow Bee 6mm to the Buff-tailed Bumblebee 22mm - exactly 1:1 on true scale on your screen.

The technical challenge was making a ruler in Power BI that reliably measures exactly 22mm on any computer monitor. You have to calculate the user's PPI (Pixels Per Inch) and Windows scaling. With some hardware parameters and quite a bit of SVG, I corrected the inner white space of the vector files. The result? A vector bumblebee that is accurate down to the millimeter on the screen.

featuredWork.title: Pokémon Analytics
featuredWork.summary: Welcome to The Ultimate Pokémon Analytics Suite! This is a custom, high-fidelity Power BI data visualization dashboard designed to break down Pokémon stats, attributes, and team combinations with a premium, arcade-style UI.
featuredWork.description:
The Ultimate Pokémon Analytics Suite
Welcome to The Ultimate Pokémon Analytics Suite! This is a custom, high-fidelity Power BI data visualization dashboard designed to break down Pokémon stats, attributes, and team combinations with a premium, arcade-style UI.

Whether you are min-maxing your competitive team or just want to see how Pikachu's speed stacks up against your favorite Pokémon, this suite turns raw pocket monster data into actionable battle intelligence.

Features
Poké Details Screen: Deep-dive into individual Pokémon metrics, base stat totals, and Body Mass Index (BMI) tracking.

Dynamic Stat Bars: Visualized attribute breakdowns covering HP, Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed.

Advanced Analytics Overlays: Custom UI components including Head-to-Head comparisons, BMI vs. Speed scatter insights, Element Distribution matrices, and Team Selection optimization.

Sleek Retro-Modern UI: Custom-themed dashboard with animated accents, custom icon wrappers, and a fully interactive sidebar selector for seamless navigation through the Pokédex.

Built With
Power BI Desktop — Core data modeling and dashboard orchestration.

DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) — Advanced data calculations, dynamic filtering, and custom KPI metrics.

HTML/CSS & SVG Canvas — Custom visuals, clean curved borders, and tailored UI element grouping.

The Pokédex API / Custom Dataset — Cleaned, optimized, and structured Pokémon attribute data.

How to Use
Clone the Repo:

Bash
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/pokemon-analytics-suite.git
Open the Report: Launch the .pbix file inside Power BI Desktop.

Explore the Data: Use the left-hand sidebar list to slice and dice stats by specific Pokémon, or toggle the bottom tabs to change your analytical perspective.

Insights & Goals
This project was built to showcase how standard business intelligence tools (like Power BI) can be pushed past their default corporate templates into highly immersive, themed user experiences. By leveraging custom canvas designs, HTML inserts, and complex data relationships, it demonstrates that data visualization can be both incredibly informative and visually striking.

featuredWork.title: Power BI Custom Visual Bubble to Bar
featuredWork.summary: Custom HTML / CSS visual for age distribution by gender. Bubble to Bar chart moving elements.
featuredWork.description:
An interactive, animated SVG population bubble chart moving towards a horizontal bar chart built entirely within a single Power BI DAX measure.

Instead of using standard, static bar charts, this custom asset renders demographic data as thousands of independent data points (particles). Upon hitting the native 'Play' button, the particles fluidly animate from a starting pool and fly to their respective age and gender categories—creating an engaging, application-like experience directly inside the report canvas.

Key features:

Native SMIL animation: Uses lightweight, built-in SVG animation paths so elements smoothly drift, overshoot, and snap into place without needing external JavaScript libraries

Interactive playback control: Features fully functional 'Play' and 'Replay' buttons coded directly into the SVG string to control the visual state on demand

Dynamic data-to-pixel mapping: Translates row counts from the model into beautifully stacked dot matrices, automatically adjusting spacing and row heights

Premium visual styling: Enhanced with glowing radial gradients, soft background bands, and distinct color transitions for gender distributions

Make it your own:
The layout boundaries, colors (currently a sleek pink and soft blue theme), animation speeds, and dot characters are driven by simple DAX variables at the top of the measure. You can quickly adapt the styling to match any brand guidelines or repurpose the particle physics for other tracking metrics.

featuredWork.title: Real-Time ISS Tracker: Fabric Eventstream & KQL Case Study
featuredWork.summary: For this project, I built an end-to-end real-time data streaming pipeline that tracks the live location of the International Space Station (ISS) and visualizes its orbital path on a dynamic dashboard. Instead of working with static datasets, this project focuses entirely on live ingestion, automated hot-storage management, and low-latency dashboarding within the Microsoft Fabric ecosystem.
featuredWork.description:
Project Overview
For this project, I built an end-to-end real-time data streaming pipeline that tracks the live location of the International Space Station (ISS) and visualizes its orbital path on a dynamic dashboard. Instead of working with static datasets, this project focuses entirely on live ingestion, automated hot-storage management, and low-latency dashboarding within the Microsoft Fabric ecosystem.

Core Architecture & Flow
Data Ingestion: A Python script deployed within a Fabric Environment continuously polls the public ISS REST API every 5 seconds, batching and pushing the live JSON data directly into a Fabric Eventstream.

Orchestration: The streaming engine is fully automated using a Microsoft Fabric Data Pipeline, allowing the process to run persistently in the background.

Real-Time Storage (KQL): The stream routes data into a Fabric Eventhouse KQL Database. Since the ISS completes one full orbit every 92 minutes, I implemented a custom database Retention Policy (softdelete = 92m) to automatically drop old telemetry points on the fly. This ensures the database always holds a perfect 'moving window' of exactly one orbit, preventing data bloating.

Live Dashboarding (Power BI): The data is surfaced via DirectQuery on an Azure Maps visualization. To bypass standard admin-enforced refresh limits, I implemented Change Detection on the timestamp column. This triggers a visual update the exact millisecond a new coordinate hits the KQL table, keeping the map seamlessly centered on the ISS.

Key Skills Demonstrated
Microsoft Fabric: Real-Time Intelligence, Eventstreams, Data Pipelines, and custom Environments.

Data Engineering: Real-time stream processing, REST API consumption, and automated data retention strategies.

KQL (Kusto Query Language): Database administration, table schema design, and retention policy mapping.

Power BI: DirectQuery optimization, advanced Azure Maps configurations, and Change Detection setup for live data.

featuredWork.title: Flow Ratio KPI Gauge Visual
featuredWork.summary: Custom HTML/CSS KPI visual for real-time backlog health monitoring.
featuredWork.description:
A bespoke HTML/CSS KPI visual within Power BI that provides the Servicedesk Team Manager with an instant, real-time pulse check on incident backlog health.

By translating the Flow Ratio-the ratio of new incoming incidents compared to resolved ones over a trailing 7-day period-into an intuitive, interactive color-coded gauge, the manager can instantly see if incoming workload and resolution output are in balance without digging into dense data tables.

Key features:
- Dynamic scale positioning using non-linear mapping across a tri-colored track
- Instant cognitive processing through dynamic colors, titles, and icons based on thresholds
- Week-over-week delta badge showing direction and amount of change
- Integrated documentation panel for seamless end-user onboarding

featuredWork.title: Chocolate Consumption vs. Nobel Prize Winners
featuredWork.summary: Does eating chocolate make a country smarter? Exploring the famous Messerli (2012) correlation (r ~= 0.88).
featuredWork.description:
An analysis of the famous correlation between per capita chocolate consumption and Nobel Prize winners, inspired by the Messerli (2012) study published in the New England Journal of Medicine.

This notebook explores a strong correlation (r ~= 0.88) between how much chocolate a country eats and how many Nobel Prize winners it produces per capita. While statistically highly significant (p < 0.0001), this is a textbook example of confounding variables - wealth and education spending drive both.

The notebook covers:
- Cross-country dataset (14 countries)
- Pearson correlation coefficient calculation
- Labeled scatter plot showing each country
- Statistical significance testing (p-value)
- Regression line with R^2
- Discussion of confounders and why correlation != causation

Key results: Pearson r = 0.88, P-value = 0.000037, R^2 = 0.77.

featuredWork.title: Power BI Landing Page - 3D Particle Animation
featuredWork.summary: Premium immersive portal with 8,000 morphing 3D particles using Three.js inside Power BI.
featuredWork.description:
A bespoke HTML/CSS/WebGL landing page within Power BI that provides users with a premium, immersive portal experience upon entering the report environment.

By translating a dynamic cloud of 8,000 independent 3D particles into a fluidly morphing visual - transitioning seamlessly between a rotating cosmic sphere and sharp typography - the asset redefines report onboarding and elevates the overall presentation of enterprise analytics.

Key features:
- High-performance 3D particle engineering using Three.js and WebGL for smooth fluid movement
- Advanced canvas pixel-to-vector mapping to transform abstract shapes into crisp, legible text elements
- Lightweight and responsive layout using CSS clamp() to ensure seamless integration across all screen sizes
- Optimized rendering architecture that maintains fluid animation speeds without impacting native report performance

Make it your own:
The code is structured to be completely modular and adaptable. The core typography, particle density, animation speeds, and the theme palette (currently configured in premium purple and mint teal) can be easily adjusted within the script to align perfectly with any corporate brand identity.

featuredWork.title: Las Vegas Weddings vs. Polar Bear Activity
featuredWork.summary: Do weddings in Vegas activate polar bears? Seasonal confounding creates a near-perfect r ~= 0.95 correlation.
featuredWork.description:
A monthly analysis showing how shared seasonality creates extremely strong but completely meaningless correlations.

This notebook explores a near-perfect correlation (r ~= 0.95) between the number of Las Vegas weddings and polar bear active hours at San Diego Zoo - both following the same seasonal cycle (peak in summer, low in winter).

This is a textbook example of seasonal confounding: two unrelated phenomena that both happen to peak in summer will always appear correlated.

The notebook covers:
- Monthly dataset (12 months)
- Pearson correlation coefficient calculation
- Dual Y-axis time series visualization
- Statistical significance testing (p-value)
- Scatter plot with regression line and month labels
- Discussion of seasonality as a confounder

Key results: Pearson r = 0.95, P-value = 0.000003, R^2 = 0.90.

The lesson: Always check for seasonal/cyclical confounders! Any two variables that peak in summer will appear highly correlated with each other.

featuredWork.title: Divorce Rate vs. Margarine Consumption
featuredWork.summary: Near-perfect spurious correlation (r ~= 0.99) between Maine divorces and US margarine consumption.
featuredWork.description:
An analysis of the near-perfect spurious correlation between the divorce rate in Maine and per capita margarine consumption in the US (2000-2009).

This notebook explores an almost perfect correlation (r ~= 0.99) between two completely unrelated variables: how many people divorce in Maine and how much margarine Americans eat. Both variables happened to decline at nearly identical rates during 2000-2009.

This is perhaps the most famous example from Tyler Vigen's collection - a powerful demonstration that even r = 0.99 with p < 0.001 means nothing without a causal mechanism.

The notebook covers:
- Dataset exploration with pandas
- Pearson correlation coefficient calculation
- Dual Y-axis time series visualization
- Statistical significance testing (p-value)
- Scatter plot with regression line
- Discussion of correlation vs. causation

Key results: Pearson r = 0.9926, P-value < 0.000001, R^2 = 0.99.
